original_reasoning: Arabic الفتيت/الفتوت = crumbled bread or grain food made by crushing/breaking with fingers. Greek ἄλφιτον = barley-groats, barley flour (grain ground/processed into coarse meal). Both refer to grain food reduced to small particles through grinding or crumbling; the masadiq level connection through crumbled grain food is strong.

Arabic الفانوس = lantern (acknowledged loan; also tale-carrier). Greek διαφανής = diaphanous, transparent, letting light through. A lantern (فانوس) is specifically an object that lets light through; διαφανής = transparent/light-permitting. The Arabic فانوس likely derives from the same root as φαίνω (to show/shine); both share a core of 'allowing light to pass through.'

Arabic القرف = bark/peel of a tree or pomegranate; skin/husk of bread that clings to a clay oven; peeled-off soil with roots. Greek κάρφος = dry arid stalk, dry twig, chip of wood. Both directly describe the dry outer surface material stripped from plants (bark, husk, wood-chip). Strong masadiq connection through plant-skin/bark domain.
Arabic خروة/خرو and Greek κίχορα (chicory) — plant name in likely shared register
shared concept: cut — masadiq direct match
Arabic rāzim=emaciated camel unable to rise; Greek marasmos=wasting away/atrophy: shared concept of progressive bodily emaciation
Arabic: drinking all in vessel. Greek: lover of drinking/drunkard — direct match.
both relate to water/waterways — clear semantic match
